introduction.p: A calculation can be correct and still leave the reader unmoved. A good mathematical essay does more. It chooses a question, follows it honestly, explains the necessary ideas, and ends with a sharper understanding than it began with.
These writing projects are not research papers in the graduate-school sense. They are mathematical explanations. The goal is to take one thread from the book and tell its story.

subsection.title: Project 1: Hamilton, quaternions, and the birth of vector algebra
subsection.p: This writing project asks you to explain that split.

A seed calculation. A pure imaginary quaternion has the form A=a_1i+a_2j+a_3k. We identify it with the vector \mathbf a=(a_1,a_2,a_3). Similarly, B=b_1i+b_2j+b_3k corresponds to \mathbf b=(b_1,b_2,b_3). Using i^2=j^2=k^2=-1, and ij=k,\qquad jk=i,\qquad ki=j, with the reversed products changing sign, one finds \boxed{AB=-\mathbf a\cdot\mathbf b+\mathbf a\times\mathbf b.} More explicitly,
AB \amp = -(a_1b_1+a_2b_2+a_3b_3) \amp \qquad + (a_2b_3-a_3b_2)i \amp \qquad + (a_3b_1-a_1b_3)j \amp \qquad + (a_1b_2-a_2b_1)k.
The scalar part is -\mathbf a\cdot\mathbf b. The vector part is \mathbf a\times\mathbf b.
So the dot product and cross product were not born as two unrelated formulas. They are two pieces of one multiplication.
Your task. Write an essay explaining how quaternions connect complex numbers, dot products, and cross products.
Your essay should answer:

li.p: Why does complex multiplication feel geometrically natural in the plane?
What changes when we try to multiply triples?
What are the rules for quaternion units i,j,k?
Why is quaternion multiplication noncommutative?
How do the dot product and cross product appear inside the product of two pure imaginary quaternions?
Why did vector algebra eventually separate the scalar part and vector part?
How does the wedge product give another way to think about oriented area?

subsection.p: What to hand in. Submit a 3- to 5-page essay with:

li.p: a title,
a clear thesis statement,
at least two worked examples,
one diagram,
a short comparison between quaternions and the vector operations used in this book,
a bibliography or source list if historical claims are included.

subsection.p: A strong essay will not only say that quaternions came before vector algebra. It will show, through a calculation, why dot and cross products are two shadows of a larger multiplication.

subsection.title: Project 2: why the cross product is both useful and suspicious
subsection.p: The cross product is one of the most useful tools in three-dimensional calculus: \mathbf u\times\mathbf v. It gives normal vectors, areas of parallelograms, torque, angular momentum, and the curl of a vector field.
But it is also suspicious.

Two vectors in the plane span an oriented parallelogram. Two vectors in space also span an oriented parallelogram. The cross product replaces that parallelogram by a perpendicular vector. This works beautifully in \mathbb R^3, because a plane has one perpendicular direction, up to sign.

But the geometric object created by two vectors is really an oriented area element, not a vector. The wedge product keeps it as an oriented area: \mathbf u\wedge\mathbf v. The cross product turns it into a vector by using extra structure: distance, angle, and orientation of space.

A seed comparison. Let \mathbf u=(1,0,0),\qquad \mathbf v=(0,2,0). The parallelogram has area 2. The cross product is \mathbf u\times\mathbf v=(0,0,2). This vector has length 2 and points in the positive z-direction.
The wedge product instead records \mathbf u\wedge\mathbf v=2\,dx\wedge dy. It says: the oriented area lies in the xy-direction and has signed size 2.
Both descriptions contain the same area information in three dimensions. But they store it differently.
Your task. Write an essay explaining the double personality of the cross product.

li.p: What geometric quantity is \norm{\mathbf u\times\mathbf v} measuring?
Why does the direction of \mathbf u\times\mathbf v depend on orientation?
Why does swapping the vectors change the sign? \mathbf v\times\mathbf u=-(\mathbf u\times\mathbf v).
Why is a surface area element more naturally written as dx\wedge dy,\qquad dy\wedge dz,\qquad dz\wedge dx than as a normal vector?
Why is the cross product especially convenient for parametrized surfaces?
What does forms language clarify about flux?

subsection.p: What to hand in. Submit a 3- to 5-page essay with:

li.p: a clear thesis,
a diagram of an oriented parallelogram,
at least three worked examples,
one paragraph defending the cross product,
one paragraph criticizing or limiting the cross product,
a concluding paragraph explaining what the wedge product changes.

subsection.p: The best essays will not treat the cross product as wrong. It is not wrong. It is a brilliant shortcut. But every shortcut hides a path, and forms reveal the path.

subsection.title: Project 3: the many lives of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
subsection.p: Single-variable calculus has a central theorem: \boxed{\int_a^b f'(x)\,dx=f(b)-f(a).} This theorem says that adding derivative information over an interval gives boundary information at the endpoints.
By the end of this book, the same sentence has grown into \boxed{\int_{\partial M}\omega=\int_Md\omega.}
This writing project asks you to tell that story.

p.md: \begin{array}{c|c|c} \text{Object }M \amp \text{Form }\omega \amp \text{Theorem} \\ \hline [a,b] \amp f \amp \displaystyle \int_{\partial[a,b]}f=\int_{[a,b]}df\\[3mm] \text{plane region }R \amp P\,dx+Q\,dy \amp \displaystyle \int_{\partial R}\omega=\int_Rd\omega\\[3mm] \text{surface }S \amp P\,dx+Q\,dy+R\,dz \amp \displaystyle \int_{\partial S}\omega=\int_Sd\omega\\[3mm] \text{solid }E \amp P\,dy\wedge dz+Q\,dz\wedge dx+R\,dx\wedge dy \amp \displaystyle \int_{\partial E}\Phi=\int_Ed\Phi \end{array}

subsection.p: In the first row, \partial[a,b]=\{b\}-\{a\}, so \int_{\partial[a,b]}f=f(b)-f(a).
In the last row, d\Phi=(P_x+Q_y+R_z)\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz, so the theorem becomes the divergence theorem.
The boundary changes dimension. The form changes degree. But the pattern does not change.

li.p: What does boundary mean for an interval, a region, a surface, and a solid?
Why do signs and orientations become unavoidable?
What changes when d acts on a 0-form, a 1-form, and a 2-form?
How do gradient, curl, and divergence fit into one operation?
Why is the statement \int_{\partial M}\omega=\int_Md\omega not just shorter, but more revealing?

subsection.p: What to hand in. Submit a 4- to 6-page essay with:

li.p: a central thesis,
a translation table,
at least three examples,
at least one diagram showing an object and its boundary,
a paragraph on orientation,
a conclusion explaining what the word fundamental means after this course.

subsection.p: A strong essay should make the reader feel that the Fundamental Theorem did not disappear after single-variable calculus. It kept changing clothes.

subsection.title: Project 4: how conservation laws unify physics and calculus
subsection.p: Chapter added time to Stokes' theorem. A conserved quantity can be stored inside a region, carried across the boundary, and created or destroyed by sources.

In forms language, the flux term becomes \int_{\partial E}\Phi_{\mathbf J}, where \Phi_{\mathbf J} = J_1\,dy\wedge dz + J_2\,dz\wedge dx + J_3\,dx\wedge dy. By Stokes' theorem, \int_{\partial E}\Phi_{\mathbf J} = \int_E d\Phi_{\mathbf J}. Since d\Phi_{\mathbf J} = (\nabla\cdot\mathbf J)\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz, the integral balance becomes, locally, \boxed{\rho_t+\nabla\cdot\mathbf J=s.} That equation is the skeleton of many physical theories.

A seed explanation: dye in water. Let \rho be dye density and let \mathbf J=\rho\mathbf v be dye flux density. If there are no sources, then s=0. The local conservation law becomes \boxed{\rho_t+\nabla\cdot(\rho\mathbf v)=0.} This says: \text{Rate of change of stored dye} + \text{Net outward flow of dye} = 0.
If more dye flows out of a small region than flows in, the density inside must decrease. If more flows in than out, the density must increase.
The equation is not a mysterious new law. It is careful accounting.
Your task. Write an essay explaining how conservation laws connect calculus to physics.

Choose one theme:
 egin{array}{c|c} 	ext{Theme} \amp 	ext{Possible law} \ \hline 	ext{Fluid or dye flow} \amp ho_t+
abla\cdot(ho\mathbf v)=0\[1mm] 	ext{Heat flow} \amp u_t=\kappa\Delta u\[1mm] 	ext{Electric charge} \amp ho_t+
abla\cdot\mathbf J=0\[1mm] 	ext{Population movement} \amp ho_t+
abla\cdot(ho\mathbf v)=s\[1mm] 	ext{Chemical concentration} \amp ho_t+
abla\cdot\mathbf J=s \end{array}
Required structure. Your essay should include:

li.p: A physical story. What is moving, stored, created, or destroyed?
A density \rho. What does it measure?
A flux \mathbf J. What does it mean physically?
A source term s, or a clear statement that s=0.
The integral balance law over a fixed region E.
The local differential equation.
An explanation of how the divergence theorem or Stokes' theorem connects the two.

subsection.p:
A useful derivation to include. Start with  rac{d}{dt}\iiint_E ho\,dV + \iint_{\partial E}\mathbf J\cdot\widehat{\mathbf n}\,dS = \iiint_E s\,dV. Use the divergence theorem: \iint_{\partial E}\mathbf J\cdot\widehat{\mathbf n}\,dS = \iiint_E
abla\cdot\mathbf J\,dV. Then \iiint_E ho_t\,dV + \iiint_E
abla\cdot\mathbf J\,dV = \iiint_E s\,dV. So \iiint_E \left( ho_t+
abla\cdot\mathbf J-s ight)dV=0. If this holds for every reasonable fixed region E, then the integrand must vanish:  oxed{ho_t+
abla\cdot\mathbf J=s.}

In forms notation, the same derivation is \frac{d}{dt}\int_E \rho\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz + \int_{\partial E}\Phi_{\mathbf J} = \int_E s\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz, then \int_E \left( \rho_t\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz + d\Phi_{\mathbf J} - s\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz \right)=0.

li.p: What does outward flux mean in your physical setting?
Why does outward flux appear with a plus sign in the balance equation but causes stored amount to decrease when there are no sources?
What does a positive source term mean?
What does the local equation say in ordinary language?
Where does Stokes' theorem enter?
Why do very different systems share the same mathematical form?

subsection.p: What to hand in. Submit a 4- to 6-page essay with:

li.p: a physical introduction,
the integral balance law,
the local differential equation,
at least one worked example with simple functions,
a diagram of a region with flux arrows crossing its boundary,
a paragraph connecting the derivation to the generalized Stokes theorem.

p.em: A short rubric for the writing projects.

conclusion.p: This chapter began as a workshop, but it ends as a mirror for the whole book. Vectors gave us direction. Derivatives gave us local change. Integrals gave us accumulation. Forms gave us the right integrands for curves, surfaces, and solids. Stokes' theorem tied boundaries to interiors.

The story is not finished. It never is. But the next time a problem asks you to measure change, flow, circulation, flux, or balance, you have a question that reaches farther than any single formula: \boxed{\text{What is the object, what is its boundary, and what form belongs on it?}}
